one_line_takeaway: A position/extended-abstract proposal to defend LLMs against prompt injection and jailbreaking by prepending trained soft prompts that suppress harmful instructions at the parameter level -- promising in concept but presented with zero empirical evaluation.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding; the paper reports no results for soft begging. Its claims are explicitly conjectural: the authors 'conjecture' that parameter-level control is inherently more effective than text-level control, and 'assume' that soft prompts are magnitudes faster to train/adapt than full model fine-tuning and can be modularized/combined per attack type. These are motivating hypotheses, not measured outcomes.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'soft begging', a conceptual defense for shielding LLMs against direct/indirect prompt injection and jailbreaking. The idea combines the naive 'begging' counter-prompt approach (asking the model to ignore harmful instructions) with parameter-efficient fine-tuning: instead of a textual counter-prompt, trainable soft-prompt vectors are prepended to every input and trained to nullify the effect of corrupted/harmful prompt portions on the output, while the LLM's own weights stay frozen. This is explicitly an extended-abstract / position paper -- it introduces the technique and the theoretical rationale but presents NO empirical evaluation of soft begging itself.
threat_model: Adversaries targeting application-integrated LLMs via (1) jailbreaking (circumventing content moderation and safety rules), (2) direct prompt injection (adversarial instructions hidden in the user prompt, sometimes visually concealed via white/tiny fonts, encoded, or API-appended), and (3) indirect prompt injection (attacker is not the user; malicious instructions are embedded in third-party documents/websites/emails consumed downstream). The paper attributes the vulnerability to the lack of an explicit separation between code/data and between valid system instructions and injected instructions in current LLMs. Consequences cited: data leakage, system manipulation, and full LLM hijacking -- especially dangerous when the LLM has autonomy or plugin/third-party access.
limitations: It is a 3-page extended abstract with no experiments, no datasets of its own, no baselines actually run, and no quantitative results -- so the central efficacy and efficiency claims remain unvalidated. The claimed advantages (parameter-level > textual control; modular combinability via prompt fusion) are asserted, not demonstrated. Practical concerns left open: whether soft begging degrades clean-task utility, robustness to adaptive or unseen injection types, the cost/accuracy of the proposed threat-identifying filter, and the fact that per-attack soft prompts still require retraining data (clean/corrupted quadruples) for each attack class. The paper's own text also idiosyncratically expands ASR as 'Attack Surface Rate' whereas the standard term is Attack Success Rate.
